data.title: PMGC Signs Letter of Intent to Purchase Custom IT Packaging Firm; Shares Fall
data.description:
PMGC Holdings said Wednesday it has signed a non-binding letter of intent to buy an unnamed US-based information technology custom packaging firm.

The target company has "close to 15 years of uninterrupted operations" and consistently delivered positive earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortisation performance year over year, PMGC said.

PMGC said it expects to sign a definitive agreement and close the deal before the end of Q2, subject to customary conditions.

The deal marks the launch of PMGC's previously announced acquisition strategy, the company said, adding this is the "first of several accretive acquisitions shareholders can expect in the months ahead."

data.title: Peru copper output flat at 216,955 tons in February
data.description:
Copper output in Peru, the world's third-largest producer of the red metal, was virtually flat in February from the same month a year ago, data from the nation's energy and mines ministry showed on Wednesday.

Output landed at 216,955 metric tons, up just 0.01% from the year-ago month, according to the ministry.

Production largely came from the MMG Ltd-owned Las Bambas nine, whose output jumped 58.4% despite two days of interrupted transit of copper concentrate, the ministry said.

Peru put out 2.74 million tons of copper last year, a slight drop from 2023 in its first decrease after four years on the rise.

Copper production in the Andean nation is expected to climb between 2% and 4% this year, Julia Torreblanca, head of Peru's national mining, oil and energy society, told Reuters last week.

Peru fell to No. 3 copper producer worldwide in 2023 after being overtaken by the Democratic Republic of Congo. Chile is the world's top producer.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1149 ET--The timeline shared by Nvidia Tuesday afternoon regarding Trump's imposed China-related restrictions for its H20 chips explains the rushed announcement that the graphic chip maker would build $500 billion of AI infrastructure over the next four years, DA Davidson analysts say in a research note. The appeal to the Trump administration was aimed at mitigating the final regulation, the analysts say. "Nvidia may now choose not to follow through on this pledge given the outcome, and if it does, we believe it will erode long-term margins," the analysts add. Nvidia shares fall 6.4% to $105.03. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1129 ET--Nvidia's $5.5 billion charge related to Trump's export restrictions to its H20 chips won't only have a significant impact on the company's first-quarter gross margins, but also its revenue growth during the entire fiscal year, William Blair analysts say in a research note. "While we still believe that Nvidia is the leader in AI infrastructure, with a significant technology moat and robust demand for its next-generation BlackWell GPUs, this will only add to the wall of worry that has built up around the name over the last few months," the analysts say. Shares may be range-bound in the near term as macro uncertainty persists, the analysts say. However, they urge investors "not to miss the forest for the trees" when it comes to a secular winner of the AI platform shift. Shares fall 6.8% to $104.61. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1041 ET - Nvidia may be using the U.S. government's new H20 license requirement as a concession in talks with the Trump administration to get the AI Diffusion Rule taken off the table, UBS analysts say in a research note. The rule, which aims to control the export and deployment of advanced chips and related technologies, is set to go into effect am May 15, according to the analysts. They say they struggle to see why Nvidia would follow through on a commitment to build $500 billion in AI infrastructure in the U.S. over the next four years if it faces both the H20 restriction, which they say is effectively a ban, and isn't able to kill the AI Diffusion Rule. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1000 ET - For the 20th consecutive month, rents declined in March, and the median asking price for rent in the 50 largest metros is now $65 lower than the 2022 peak, standing at $1,694, according to Realtor.com. While rents have been declining for nearly two years due in large part to new multi-family inventory, new tariffs could have impacts on metros where multi-family permitting activity is growing the fastest, jeopardizing rent declines. "In nearly every major U.S. metro, rents are still considerably higher than 2019," says Realtor.com. The recently announced tariffs on imported building materials such as steel and aluminum could potentially impact the multi-family housing supply by driving up construction costs. Markets that experienced rapid growth in permitted multi-family homes are expected to see the biggest impacts as developers and builders may postpone or even cancel new projects. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0828 ET - Wedbush says the Trump administration's move to restrict next generation AI chips to China is a "strategic blow" for Nvidia and that the company "now has massive blockades going after the China market in the middle of this raging US/China tariff battle." The analysts say in a note that "the Street will take this news with clear nervousness worried these are the first shots fired in the tech battle between the US and China and Beijing/Xi are not just going to take this news and walk away." Nvidia slides 6.4% to $104.94 in premarket trading. Chip stocks in general are all lower this morning with Broadcom, Qualcomm, Micron, Marvell, AMD all weaker. ASML Holding is also lower after 2Q sales guidance came up short.(denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0546 ET - Ericsson should be a good relative performer in 2025 given its exposure to more stable telecom operator spending and given the risks to other hardware stocks, JPMorgan analyst Sandeep Deshpande writes. That said, margin risks have increased as the high-margin U.S. market represented 45% of revenue in the first quarter and it's not clear how much customers increased their buying ahead of tariffs. The pre-buy effect could reverse in the second half, he says. Second-quarter margin guidance is better than expectations, aided by a partial Lenovo contract settlement. Given the radio access network market is unlikely to grow in the next few years, investor focus will be on returns, not growth. "Ericsson could make the stock really interesting with a buyback." Shares fall 0.9%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0501 ET - Investors prefer U.K. stocks over U.S. stocks, Bank of America's global fund managers survey for April shows. Investors also favor bonds, utilities and staples, while they are underweight the tech sector, the energy sector and industrials, according to the survey. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0449 ET - Bitcoin edges lower after Nvidia said it faces a $5.5 billion hit from tighter U.S. rules on shipments of its new artificial intelligence chip to China. The microchip maker will require licences to export its chip to China as the U.S.-China trade war escalates. "Trump also launched a probe into whether critical minerals should face tariffs, so that added to fears that further tariffs were on the horizon," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. That raises fears that the trade war could still get worse from here, they say. Bitcoin falls 0.9% to $83,215, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0427 ET - New restrictions on Nvidia's H20 chips may further support China's efforts to locally source chips, according to Citi research analysts. "While the new restriction is not a definite ban, the license requirement adds to H20 supply uncertainties and is mildly positive for China's domestic AI chips and the supply chain," they say. The analysts expect Huawei's Ascend 910B/910C to account for a significant portion of China's AI accelerator supply this year. In the event of a complete ban on Nvidia's H20 chips, China's advanced manufacturing capacity-- at the 7nm-equivalent level or more advanced--should be sufficient to support the production of domestic AI chips this year, the analysts say. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0424 ET - 'Long gold' ends a 24-month streak for 'long Magnificent 7' as the most crowded trade, Bank of America's April global fund manager survey shows. 'Long gold' is cited as the most crowded trade by 49% of investors surveyed by BofA, followed by 24% mentioning 'long Magnificent 7', a reference to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Meta and Tesla stocks. Gold is also expected to be the best performing asset in 2025 according to 42% of investors, up from 23% in March. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0417 ET - ASML Holding's reiteration that the Dutch semiconductor-equipment maker will grow in 2025 and 2026 is a positive sign amid uncertainty from President Trump's fast-changing tariff decisions, Jefferies analysts write in a research note. The company said it was working to minimize the impact of any tariffs on its systems and component shipments as much as possible, the analysts note. ASML forecasts between 30 billion euros and 35 billion euros in sales this year. ASML shares trade 6.1% lower after first-quarter orders of 3.94 billion euros came in below a Visible Alpha consensus of 4.84 billion euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0236 ET - ASML Holding's guidance for the current quarter is below market expectations, Citi analysts write in a research note. The Dutch semiconductor-equipment maker expects sales between 7.2 billion and 7.7 billion euros and a gross margin between 50% and 53%, a range it said was larger than usual due to uncertainty around tariffs. The sales guidance midpoint of 7.45 billion euros is below consensus of 7.77 billion euros, the analysts note. Meanwhile, the gross margin midpoint is about 91 basis points below consensus of 52.4%, they add. For the year, ASML continues to expect between 30 billion and 35 billion euros in sales. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

data.title: Prison Phone Company Aventiv Technologies Confirms Restructuring Via Debt-for-Equity Swap
Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1108 ET - Abbott Laboratories management considered raising earnings guidance for the year given the momentum they were seeing in the company's base business, but opted to reaffirm the outlook in light of uncertainty around President Trump's tariffs, CEO Robert Ford says on a call with analysts. The company estimates that the tariffs could have an impact of "a few hundred million dollars," Ford says, with most of that coming in the back half of the year. He expects there might be some mitigating factors offsetting that impact, including a weakening U.S. dollar or other foreign exchange shifts. "I can tell you there's not a lot of R&D slowing down or SG&A slowing down in those mitigation plans," the CEO says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0640 ET - Fewer investors find European bank stocks more attractive than they did before April, Bank of America's European fund manager survey for April shows. Investors prefer defensive stocks such as utilities and healthcare. The April survey shows 30% of investors find European banks attractive, down from 50% in March. A 26% share of investors expect European banks to face pressure, up from 13% in March. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0442 ET - Sartorius's 1Q beat was driven by strong consumables sales, whereas equipment sales remained soft, Intron Health analysts say in a note. While the analysts ponder whether tariff-driven customer stocking played a role, all major regions excluding China contributed at least high single-digit growth. Shares in the German life-science company rise 5.9% to 205.60 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0435 ET - The unquantified beat in Sartorius's bioprocess solutions orders, a critical metric for investors, is a major positive, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. The German life science company's 2025 outlook provides limited insight into the potential effects of tariffs on margins but suggests a limited financial impact. Its 1Q results are a continuation of trends from 2H 2024, in line with recent management commentary during its annual results, the analysts say. Shares rise 2.6% to 199.25 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0412 ET - Sartorius's better-than-expected 1Q results and a quantified 2025 outlook reassure investors, reflecting a solid market recovery, Jefferies analysts say in a note. The German life-science company's issued 2025 guidance of 6% sales growth and underlying Ebitda margin of around 29%-30%, which should be well-received by investors, they say. Shares are up 3.2% at 200.50 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0257 ET - Praram 9 Hospital likely has high earnings growth with potential upside, Maybank Securities (Thailand)'s Nontapat Sahakitpinyo says in a research report. The Thailand hospital operator should deliver decent earnings in 1Q, supported by strong international revenue, the analyst says. Its 2Q momentum should remain robust on a yearly and quarterly basis, underpinned by Middle East patient inflows post-Ramadan, the analyst says. A tax benefit of THB15 million is also expected in 2Q or 3Q from medical equipment donations to public hospitals, the analyst adds. The brokerage has a buy rating and a target price of THB32.00 on the shares, which are 3.6% higher at THB23.30. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0255 ET - Ipsen's first-quarter sales came in ahead of consensus expectations after a better-than-expected result for its Somatuline cancer drug, RBC Capital Markets analysts say in a research note. The drug's sales were robust, benefiting from continuing shortage of generics competitors, and beat expectations by 14%, according to RBC. "Clearly, it is likely that supply improves through the year and thus sales could still move into a decline in [the second half], but this is a good start," the analysts say. The outlook for Somatuline and potential impacts from U.S. tariffs are likely to be in focus at the company's earnings call, RBC says. Ipsen reiterated its full-year guidance. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

2139 ET - UOB KayHian cuts its year-end KLCI target to 1620 from 1690, citing persistent U.S.-China tariff tensions, high U.S. bond yields, and potential "Trump tantrums" that weigh on valuations and recession risks. Although Trump rolled back extreme tariff threats, escalating trade frictions may still dampen sentiment, analysts say in a note. They remain defensive but see near-term trading opportunities. Tech remains vulnerable, but relief on phones, PCs, and chips opens tactical entry points in oversold names like Inari Amertron and NationGate, they say. Malaysia's lower tariff exposure also supports trade diversion, likely benefiting electronic manufacturing services players, UOB KH adds. Top picks include Eco World Development, Gamuda, Hartalega, and Hong Leong Bank. The KLCI is flat at 1486.60. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1452 ET - Johnson & Johnson's 1Q report bodes well for the medical-technology sector, JPMorgan analysts say in a research note, citing the company's positive commentary on overall demand and preliminary thoughts on tariffs. J&J's sales of medical devices came in slightly below expectations; however, management highlighted a normalized procedure environment, no recession signaling in the near-term as elective procedure growth remains steady and an expectation for sales to grow in the back half of the year. The company expects a $400 million hit from tariffs, though the analysts note that J&J has some of the highest exposure to China in the medical-technology sector, at about 10%. "Overall, we view the totality of today's updates as a positive for the rest of MedTech earnings," the analysts write. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1209 ET - Interactive Brokers has boosted its crypto offerings after the Securities and Exchange Commission loosened its stance on the industry, Chief Executive Milan Gilak said during the company's earnings call. But with its expanded offerings, Interactive Brokers isn't seeing as much interest in the space as expected, he said. "It's not growing as fast as we would like, which to me personally is somewhat of a surprise," he said, adding the cost of trading crypto assets on its platform is lower than what its competitors charge. The company added four new cryptocurrencies last quarter and three more this week, bringing its total offering to 11 cryptocurrencies. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1101 ET - Travelers says the direct impact from tariffs enacted by the Trump administration should be pretty manageable. CEO Alan Schnitzer on an analyst call says the impact is "just a fraction of auto and property losses that are physical damage related and only a fraction of those are for materials that would be impacted by tariffs." He adds that the most significant affect for the company is likely to be one-time impacts of physical damage repair costs, which most notably affects private passenger auto. "From there, the impacts diminish pretty significantly." Shares rise 3.9% to $259.47 following a 1Q earnings beat despite losses from California wildfires. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1038 ET - European insurers form a relatively defensive subsector and would be resilient to a recession, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note. "Our constructive view is supported by 'staple like' demand, continued earnings power and healthy balance sheets," its analysts write, noting that insurers have tightened their risk management and pricing discipline in recent years. European asset managers are, conversely, among the worst-performing names. This is due to the impact on their assets under management, which is tied to their earnings, and the sentiment turning more risk-off, RBC adds. Investor reticence to gain exposure in this space for now is understandable, the analysts say.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0640 ET - Fewer investors find European bank stocks more attractive than they did before April, Bank of America's European fund manager survey for April shows. Investors prefer defensive stocks such as utilities and healthcare. The April survey shows 30% of investors find European banks attractive, down from 50% in March. A 26% share of investors expect European banks to face pressure, up from 13% in March. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0501 ET - Investors prefer U.K. stocks over U.S. stocks, Bank of America's global fund managers survey for April shows. Investors also favor bonds, utilities and staples, while they are underweight the tech sector, the energy sector and industrials, according to the survey. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0446 ET - Wise could face revenue hurdles under the possibility of higher U.S. tariffs, Peel hunt analysts Gautam Pillai and Barun Singh write in a note. This might lead to a widespread growth and consumer spending slowdown, the analysts say. "The company remains inherently consumer-focused, with a significant share of personal cross-border flows tied to discretionary spending categories such as travel, holidays, and overseas gifting." Any pullback in these categories can weigh on transaction volumes and hurt the money-transfer company's top-line growth, they say. Shares are down 1.6% at 955.50 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0351 ET - Italy's opinion on whether to use its so-called golden powers to allow UniCredit's takeover of smaller peer Banco BPM could come earlier than expected and with conditions, Equita says in a research note after two Italian newspapers reported the decision could land in the next few days. A conditional approval is reportedly likely, with limits on branch reductions and on the rationalization of investment banking activities, guarantees on its headquarters' location and a potential exit from Russia. "These elements do not, in our opinion, diminish the industrial sense of the deal; however, it will be necessary to understand their magnitude in detail in order to properly assess the impact on the generation of synergies and value creation from the transaction," analyst Andrea Lisi writes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0304 ET - EQT shares could see an initial positive reaction to strong comments around BPEA fundraising, especially given the high short interest, JPMorgan analysts write. However, the outlook on exits, fund valuations and fundraising could potentially drive consensus EPS downgrades. "Hence we remain cautious." The company had a strong first quarter, but the outlook points to slowdown. EQT's fee-generating assets under management rose to 142 billion euros, 3% above consensus on stronger fundraising of 12.4 billion euros. This was driven by the activation of BPEA IX fund earlier than expected, JPMorgan says. EQT now expects a "material slowdown" in exit activity, its listed holdings have declined by 9% quarter-to-date, and industry-wide fundraising volumes are not expected to return to 2021 levels until 2027. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0241 ET - Nordea reported another solid result with pre-provision profits coming in 4% ahead of consensus, Jefferies analyst Alexander Demetriou writes. A positive was the 2% beat on net interest income, with volumes coming in better than expected, he says. Fees were below while costs were better. Pretax profit came in 7% ahead and the CET1 ratio of 15.7% was 20 basis points ahead, he says. Nordea continues to target a return on equity of above 15% in 2025 with consensus currently at 14.5%. Jefferies rates Nordea at buy with a 13 euro price target. Shares closed at 11.15 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

2139 ET - UOB KayHian cuts its year-end KLCI target to 1620 from 1690, citing persistent U.S.-China tariff tensions, high U.S. bond yields, and potential "Trump tantrums" that weigh on valuations and recession risks. Although Trump rolled back extreme tariff threats, escalating trade frictions may still dampen sentiment, analysts say in a note. They remain defensive but see near-term trading opportunities. Tech remains vulnerable, but relief on phones, PCs, and chips opens tactical entry points in oversold names like Inari Amertron and NationGate, they say. Malaysia's lower tariff exposure also supports trade diversion, likely benefiting electronic manufacturing services players, UOB KH adds. Top picks include Eco World Development, Gamuda, Hartalega, and Hong Leong Bank. The KLCI is flat at 1486.60. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

1429 ET - U.S. cyber insurance renewal premium rates, which have fallen year-over-year for the last three quarters, are expected to remain under pressure near-term, barring a significant event, Fitch Ratings says. "The cyber insurance line was profitable for 2024, but direct written premiums fell for the second straight year due to declining rates and fewer policies in force," according to Fitch. Ongoing challenges include intensifying market competition, as well as "an evolving claims environment increasingly influenced by technological changes, including artificial intelligence, rather than regulations or case law," Fitch adds. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1236 ET - U.S. beverages and household and personal care companies will report challenging 1Q results, Citi analysts say in a research note. The companies aren't only facing a difficult set-up for the sector with soft commentary on the U.S. and Europe, but also from key emerging markets such as Mexico and India. Both markets had been positive elements of growth for the companies in the past few quarters. Many haven't provided commentary on tariffs, so that will be a large focus. Citi has a cautious near-term view on Procter & Gamble, Colgate-Palmolive and Coca-Cola, and a positive view on Church & Dwight, Boston Beer and Keurig Dr Pepper. Citi cuts its target price for the stocks of Estee Lauder, Newell Brands, Clorox and Brown-Forman, and raises its target price on Boston Beer, Monster Beverage and Kenvue. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0959 ET - With Parkland's CEO stepping down one day after shareholder Simpson Oil again called for an overhaul of the fuel distributor and retailer, TD Cowen says Simpson's hand is now even stronger going into the May 6 shareholder meeting. Simpson is battling to elect an alternate slate of directors. Analyst Michael Van Aelst says that Bob Espey's tenure was one of Simpson's sticking points and, coupled with another earnings miss, could clear the way for Simpson to gain control of the board. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0919 ET - Crude futures extend gains, supported by dollar weakness and the market encouraged by a Bloomberg report that China would negotiate with the U.S. on tariffs under certain conditions. Macroeconomic headwinds will likely limit price recovery despite some optimism around potential tariff rollbacks, while rising U.S. crude inventories suggest ample supply, George Pavel of Naga.com Middle East says in a note. "Without stronger support from broader market conditions, crude oil prices could remain near their lows," he says. The API reported a 2.4 million barrel crude stock build for last week, analysts note. Closely watched EIA inventory numbers are due at 10:30 a.m. ET. WTI and Brent are up 1.1% at $62.01 and $65.38 a barrel, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0640 ET - Fewer investors find European bank stocks more attractive than they did before April, Bank of America's European fund manager survey for April shows. Investors prefer defensive stocks such as utilities and healthcare. The April survey shows 30% of investors find European banks attractive, down from 50% in March. A 26% share of investors expect European banks to face pressure, up from 13% in March. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0501 ET - Investors prefer U.K. stocks over U.S. stocks, Bank of America's global fund managers survey for April shows. Investors also favor bonds, utilities and staples, while they are underweight the tech sector, the energy sector and industrials, according to the survey. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0341 ET - Allocation to bonds shows a record increase, according to Bank of America's April global fund manager survey. Relative to history, investors are overweight bonds, but also to utilities, staples and U.K. stocks, BofA says in an accompanying press statement. The rush to bonds is caused by U.S. capital flight and global fiscal panic. Alongside the record increase in allocation to bonds, global equity allocation fell to its lowest since July 2023. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

2009 ET - Oil prices edge higher in the early Asian session on possible position adjustments. However, dimming prospects of trade deals amid the global tariff conflict may weigh. Negotiations between the EU and the U.S. have made little progress toward a deal, ANZ Research analysts say in a research report. Also, "Trump administration officials have indicated that the 20% reciprocal tariff, as well as other tariffs targeting sectors such as cars and metals, will not be removed outright," the analysts add. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are up 0.2% at $61.47/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.2% higher at $64.82/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1505 ET - Oil futures tread water for much of the session and settle lower as markets appear to steady after two weeks of tariff-driven volatility. "Macro sentiment has finally stabilized, which has helped stabilize oil as well. Crude will likely tie closer to the fundamental landscape," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. Future demand remains at the forefront of trader concerns, with the IEA cutting its 2025 forecast to around 730,000 barrels a day from just over 1 million b/d, following demand downgrades by OPEC and the U.S. EIA. Meanwhile, the Keystone pipeline that carries Canadian oil to the U.S. Midwest restarted following repairs after last week's spill, the Pipeline Safety and Hazardous Materials Administration said. WTI settles down 0.3% at $61.33 a barrel and Brent falls 0.3% to $64.67 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0525 ET - Rio Tinto's 1Q copper production was 6% above consensus expectations due to higher grades at its Escondida mine, while bauxite production was 5% above due to a strong performance in Guinea, Barclays analysts write. Overall, the miner delivered a soft quarter as Australian shipments of iron ore were weak due to losses sustained from four cyclones, they write. Its London-listed shares trade down 1.2% at 4,299 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0424 ET - 'Long gold' ends a 24-month streak for 'long Magnificent 7' as the most crowded trade, Bank of America's April global fund manager survey shows. 'Long gold' is cited as the most crowded trade by 49% of investors surveyed by BofA, followed by 24% mentioning 'long Magnificent 7', a reference to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Meta and Tesla stocks. Gold is also expected to be the best performing asset in 2025 according to 42% of investors, up from 23% in March. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0315 ET - Antofagasta's first-quarter copper production was 2% lower than expected due to a weaker performance at its Los Pelambres mine, which was impacted by a national power outage and maintenance, RBC Capital Markets analysts Ben Davis and Marina Calero write. Its average copper price $4.69 a pound was 13% higher than expected, they write. Antofagasta's permit for its Zaldivar mine is due to expire in May and its renewal is going to the wire, however, there is no indications that it shouldn't be received in time, they add. Shares fall 2.2% at 1,502 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

2237 ET - Rio Tinto bull Jefferies says it is a buyer of the miner's stock at current prices, despite a clearly challenging 1Q operationally and risks from Trump's trade war. It says Rio's Australia-listed stock trades at an EV/Ebitda of 5.0X based on Jefferies's 2026 estimates and a free cash flow yield of 6.4%. "We would expect Rio shares to be defensive as market volatility likely persists in the near term," Jefferies analysts say in a note. "These shares also offer leverage to an eventual cyclical recovery, although that probably doesn't happen until 2026." Rio Tinto is down 1.7% in Sydney at A$109.51 after reporting a fall in 1Q iron-ore shipments. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2103 ET - Rio Tinto's 1Q Pilbara iron-ore shipments were impacted by cyclones more than Citi expected, says analyst Paul McTaggart. Shipments of 70.7 million metric tons are down 17% on the quarter immediately prior, while the lower-grade SP10 product represents "a high" 32% of shipments, he says. Iron ore contributes most of Rio Tinto's profits. Production of other commodities is broadly in line, "but noting Escondida refined copper production was impacted by the Chile-wide power outage," McTaggart says. He adds that "lithium was a one-liner for Rio in this report," following its recent acquisition of Arcadium Lithium. Citi has a neutral rating and A$130.00 target on Rio Tinto, which is down 1.3% at A$109.94. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1414 GMT - United Airlines' earnings indicated stability in forward bookings, giving analysts hope the company can withstand fears of an economic downturn. Several airlines said in March they were seeing falling consumer demand due to economic uncertainty, but United said Tuesday advanced ticket sales rose by almost $3 billion from the prior quarter, a good sign for forward bookings, TD Cowen analysts Tom Fitzgerald and Helane Becker say. Premium and international booking are driving that with 17% and 5% growth respectively. While holiday travel could be helping the bookings figures, the numbers are still solid as investors look for a potential deterioration in international booking from tariffs, BofA analyst Andrew Didora says. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1254 GMT - Treasury yields are little changed from yesterday's settle after March retail sales beat expectations. Sales increased 1.4%, while economists surveyed by WSJ expected 1.2%. February's figure was unrevised at 0.2%. Sales ex-autos rose 0.5%, versus consensus of 0.3%. The data may reflect consumers anticipating purchases to get ahead of tariffs. Industrial production data is due at 9:15 a.m. ET. Fed Chair Powell has a speech at 1:30 p.m. and investors will likely watch for hints on how tariffs might be affecting interest rate decisions. The 10-year yield is at 4.326% and the two-year at 3.812%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0930 GMT - BMW could be one of the first names that investors consider to play any bounce in European autos, Citi analysts write. The bank has been cautious for the past few years, firstly as BMW was over-earning during Covid, and latterly due to BMW's high China exposure. Whilst BMW's 5%-7% auto EBIT margin guidance is encouraging, the 17% first quarter decline in China sales highlights continued pressure. Nevertheless, BMW had a positive pre-earnings call, shares are down 13% since tariffs began, and it holds around 70 euros per share in net cash. "Whilst we prefer Renault and Volkswagen on fundamental EPS momentum, we think BMW could lift from current levels as tariff overreaction recedes." Shares fall 1.2%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0913 GMT - XPeng is expected to further develop its autonomous driving technology, Nomura analysts write in a note. The entire auto market in China is likely to enter the "autonomous driving 3.0 era" from the 2.0 era, they add. XPeng also targets mass production of its flying cars and humanoid robots within 2026, they say. The technology and features updates will likely support XPeng in maintaining its market momentum into the rest of this year, Nomura says. The company showed an active business plan, with the upcoming G7 and Mona M03 Max in addition to its G01 model, they say. Nomura maintains a positive stance on XPeng's outlook for the foreseeable future. The company could see some sequential improvement for its monthly sales and further share gains in China, they add. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0907 GMT - Malaysia's auto sector could face subdued growth prospects amid intensifying competition and currency volatility, CIMB Securities analyst Mohd Shanaz Noor Azam says in a note. A prolonged U.S.-China trade conflict could weaken the ringgit and lift input costs, straining margins and dampening big-ticket spending like car purchases, he reckons. Chinese EV makers are expected to ramp up their presence in Malaysia, raising pressure on local and non-Chinese foreign brands, he adds. However, national brands like Perodua are seen as more resilient due to affordability and their high proportion of local content, Shanaz says. CIMB maintains a neutral rating on Malaysia's auto sector, pegging Sime Darby as its top pick. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0824 GMT - 'Long gold' ends a 24-month streak for 'long Magnificent 7' as the most crowded trade, Bank of America's April global fund manager survey shows. 'Long gold' is cited as the most crowded trade by 49% of investors surveyed by BofA, followed by 24% mentioning 'long Magnificent 7', a reference to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Meta and Tesla stocks. Gold is also expected to be the best performing asset in 2025 according to 42% of investors, up from 23% in March. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0640 GMT - Maersk shares are attractively valued despite consensus assuming unprecedented negativity in the ocean unit, Citi analysts write. The stock is cheap on many valuation metrics, and while it's not cheap using the P/E measure, this is because the outlook for ocean is challenging, it adds. In Citi's view, the market may be overlooking Maersk swinging from net debt of $12.5 billion at the end of 2019 to net cash of $6.4 billion at the end of 2024. The market is concerned about trade demand and excess capacity, but Citi notes the potential for higher levels of vessel scrapping and idling and greater responsiveness to challenging conditions. Citi upgrades Maersk to neutral from sell and raises its target price to 11,093 Danish kroner from 9,500 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0640 GMT - China's domestic market for autos will continue to outshine this year, thanks to an effective government stimulus, Daiwa analysts write in a note. The Shanghai Auto Show in late April is a near-term catalyst, they say. Daiwa revises up its China EV outlook this year, driven by stronger-than-expected battery EV demand. Daiwa now expects China's EV market to grow 33% on year. The current U.S. tariffs on auto imports from China remain applicable, they say. Some China's auto component names may benefit from the potential pause of a 25% tariff on the auto industry in the near term, Daiwa says. The brokerage expects limited impact from tariffs on Chinese automakers as most have already given up their U.S. market since China-U.S. trade war started. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0129 GMT - HL Mando's 1Q results were likely dampened by limited output increases by its local auto maker clients, Daiwa Capital analysts Yoonki Bae and Henny Jung write in a note. The South Korean car-component supplier may also have suffered from production line adjustments at U.S. electric-vehicle makers, the analysts say. Mando's 1Q revenue and operating profit likely fell 10% and 27%, respectively, on quarter, they note. Still, they remain optimistic about Mando's 2025 growth. The impact of U.S. tariffs on Mando will be minimal as its American clients are responsible for import clearance, they add. Daiwa cuts the stock's target price by 9% to KRW50,000 but keeps a buy rating. Shares are 2.7% lower at KRW36,500. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2124 GMT - United Airlines said air-travel demand is weaker but stable — and that weakness is mainly coming from domestic travel. The airline said domestic passenger revenue per available seat mile (PRASM) fell 4% in the first quarter and domestic passenger load slipped 3.4 percentage points. Meanwhile, international PRASM climbed 5% and international passenger load rose almost two percentage points. Forward bookings over the past two weeks have stayed stable, United said, with international up 5%. CEO Scott Kirby said in March that a 50% drop in government-related travel had rippled into domestic leisure travel, which was also on a decline. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

2117 GMT - United Airlines is relying on its loyalty program to stay resilient through a potential economic downturn, it said in its earnings report. The airline said revenue from its premium-cabin seating rose 9.2% in the first quarter despite what it called a challenging macroeconomic environment. That was compared with 7% to 8% growth in business and basic-economy revenue. Focusing on brand loyalty has been United's strategy since Covid, it said, and it intends to continue accelerating investments there. "United believes our proven ability to win brand-loyal customers is a competitive advantage and will make United resilient in any economic environment," it said. Shares rise 5.9% in late trading. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

2116 GMT - United Airlines shared two financial outlooks for its full-year earnings because it believes it is "impossible to predict" how the economy will shape up during the rest of the year. The first outlook, which is the same range it previously shared in January, is based on a stable economic scenario where books remain weaker but stable. If the U.S. enters a recession, United is modeling an incremental five-point reduction to total operating revenue, further capacity reductions and a lower adjusted earnings range. "A single consensus no longer exists, and therefore the Company's expectation has become bimodal," United said. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Italy's Cucinelli maintains sales growth forecasts for this year and next
data.description:
Italian luxury fashion group Brunello Cucinelli on Wednesday confirmed its expectations for sales growth of around 10% in both 2025 and 2026 despite looming U.S. tariffs.

Starting from its Fall-Winter 2025 collections, the company will work on a new price list in the U.S. in response to the possible impact of tariffs but it does not expect this to have much impact on its business.

"We do not believe that this measure will result in a significant change in purchases by American clients," it said in a statement, noting the strength of its brand.

The group said sales had risen 10.5% at constant exchange rates in the first quarter, boosted by a solid performance in retail and wholesale channels.

Best known for its cashmere garments, the Italian company sold 37% of its products in the Americas region last year.

First quarter sales came in at 341.5 million euros ($388.2 million), broadly in line with an analysts' forecast of 343 million euros, according to LSEG data.

Amid a slowdown in luxury demand, the group has so far outperformed the industry thanks to its high-end positioning.

($1 = 0.8797 euros)

data.title: Update: Orezone Gold Up 9% as Q1 Gold Production Declines, Provides Hard Rock Expansion Update
data.description:
(Updates shares.)

Orezone Gold on Wednesday said first-quarter gold production fell to 28,688 ounces compared with 30,139 ounces in the same quarter of 2024.

Gold sales declined to 28,943 ounces from 31,229 ounces with an average realized price of US$2,851 per ounce for sales of US$82.5 million, up from US$2,066 per ounce for sales of US$64.5 million.

The company said it is positioned to achieve its 2025 production guidance of 115,000 to 130,000 ounces.

"Q1 was another solid operating quarter at Bombore, with slightly lower than planned gold ounces produced as a result of re-scheduled mill maintenance," said chief executive Patrick Downey.

Construction of the Stage I hard rock expansion remains ahead of schedule and on budget. First gold pour and mill commissioning is on track for the fourth quarter. Engineering is ahead of schedule with 85% progress to the end of March.

Completion of the expansion is expected to result in gold production increasing by about 45% from current levels to 170,000 to 185,000 ounces in 2026.

During the quarter, Orezone announced plans to advance a secondary listing on the Australian Securities Exchange, with a target listing in mid-2025. It is also evaluating plans to accelerate the Stage II hard rock expansion to an overall 5 million tonnes per annum two years ahead of schedule.

The Stage II expansion is forecast to increase overall gold production at Bombore to 220,000 to 250,000 ounces per year.

Orezone's share were last seen up $0.10 to $1.26 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: Cherry AI: A Revenue-Backed Infrastructure Layer Positioned for a Breakout IDO
data.description:
Dubai, UAE, April 16, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — In a market saturated with underperforming tokens and fragmented utility models, few Web3 projects demonstrate early-stage profitability, product depth, and embedded user distribution prior to their token launch. Cherry AI stands out as a data-rich infrastructure layer with $5 million in on-chain revenue since late 2024, now heading into its public IDO on April 23 with an expanding product suite and broad developer adoption. Infrastructure First: Scalable Tooling Across Telegram

Cherry AI provides an end-to-end stack of Telegram-native tools for community management, token discovery, and automated trading—built to support project teams, token launches, and user engagement in Web3-native environments. Its flagship product, Cherry Bot, is currently installed in over 150,000 Telegram groups and actively used by 110,000+ Web3 communities. It powers advanced buy bot alerts, moderation, raid tools, group portals, and real-time community analytics.

The platform also includes Cherry Trending, an AI-powered token ranking system that aggregates metrics like volume growth, liquidity shifts, social sentiment, and new holder activity across Solana, Ethereum, Base, BNB Chain, and other major chains. Meanwhile, the newly launched Cherry Trading Bot provides high-speed wallet generation, real-time buy/sell execution, limit orders, DCA automation, and more—all accessible directly inside Telegram.

With these systems integrated into an audience of over 12 million users, Cherry AI has positioned itself not as a speculative protocol—but as a core operating layer within the Telegram-centric crypto economy. $CHERRY Tokenomics: Utility-Driven, Revenue-Linked

Cherry’s token, $CHERRY, is tied directly to platform economics—functioning as a transaction layer and distribution mechanism across its core products. Rather than preemptive inflation, Cherry’s design redirects real usage into token flows, allocating 50% of all platform revenue as follows:

20%: Automated token buybacks and burns 

20%: Cherry Bomb – a reward engine funding raids, quests, and user incentives 

10%: Directly into the staking rewards pool

The remaining revenue funds operations, partnerships, and continued product development. With peak monthly revenue crossing $1M, these mechanics introduce a deflationary system backed by recurring cash flows—not just speculative demand.

Importantly, Cherry Launchpad is now live and will host the public sale of $CHERRY. The launchpad also serves as a new revenue source, onboarding vetted early-stage projects and offering $CHERRY holders access to curated deal flow. Market Fit and Institutional Consideration

Cherry AI’s value lies in its infrastructure footprint—deep integrations into the most active and overlooked distribution channel in crypto: Telegram. With launchpads, meme coins, trader groups, and influencers relying on Cherry's tooling, the platform effectively powers front-end community engagement across thousands of projects.

This gives it a distribution advantage rarely seen in early-stage Web3 plays. While competitors rely on hype-driven token mechanics, Cherry leverages operational infrastructure and revenue loops that continuously reinforce token value through embedded usage.

From an institutional perspective, Cherry’s growth model mirrors a scaled SaaS framework: multiple modular tools, high-margin monetization, and network-driven usage. For hedge funds and digital asset managers seeking exposure to Web3 infrastructure with cash flow potential, Cherry AI presents a structurally unique thesis. Risks and Outlook

Key risks include continued reliance on Telegram as a central platform layer, evolving token regulation, and long-term user retention amidst tooling competition. However, Cherry’s already-live stack, monetization history, and expanding roadmap mitigate many of the typical execution risks associated with pre-launch token offerings.

The IDO comes at a $20M valuation, while current business fundamentals support a valuation exceeding $50M—a rare opportunity to invest at a significant discount. Cherry offers a rare alignment of profitability, active distribution, and undervalued access. By year-end, internal projections suggest $10M–$15M in annualized revenue, with token buybacks potentially outpacing circulating inflation across comparable small-cap tokens.

data.title: European Equities Close Mixed in Wednesday Trading; Euro Area Inflation Declines to 2.2%
data.description:
The European stock markets closed mixed in Wednesday trading as The Stoxx Europe 600 was down 0.19%, Germany's DAX gained 0.29%, the FTSE 100 in London was up 0.32%, France's CAC 40 was off 0.07%, and the Swiss Market Index declined 0.10%.

The euro area annual inflation rate was 2.2% in March, down from 2.3% in February and 2.4% a year earlier, while European Union annual inflation declined to 2.5% from 2.7% in February and 2.6% a year earlier, according to Eurostat, the statistical office of the EU.

The lowest annual rates were in France (0.9%), Denmark (1.4%) and Luxembourg (1.5%), while the highest annual rates were in Romania (5.1%), Hungary (4.8%) and Poland (4.4%). Compared with February, annual inflation declined in 16 member states, rose in 10, and remained unchanged in one.

In the UK, annual inflation declined to 2.6% in March from 2.8% the previous month, according to the Office for National Statistics. The ONS said the largest downward contributions to inflation came from recreation and culture, and motor fuels.

And in corporate news, shares of Bunzl plunged 25% on the FTSE in London after the UK distribution and outsourcing company said Wednesday that it has lowered its 2025 forecast and paused its share buyback program. The company said it had to reduce its guidance due to "operational challenges faced by our largest business in North America."

Rio Tinto late Tuesday reported Q1 iron ore shipments of 70.7 million metric tons from the Pilbara region in Australia, down 9% from a year earlier. Bauxite production for the quarter rose 12% year over year to 15 million metric tons.

Shares of the British mining giant were flat in London trading.

data.title: Nvidia Could See Hit to Financials Amid Restrictions on Chip Exports to China, Morgan Stanley Says
data.description:
The US government's export restrictions on a chip made by Nvidia could be "disruptive" to its financials, Morgan Stanley said in a note emailed Wednesday, after the chipmaker said it would take up to $5.5 billion in related charges.

In a late Tuesday filing, Nvidia said that the government requires a license to export H20 integrated circuits to China and "D:5 countries." The restrictions also apply to "any other circuits achieving the H20's memory bandwidth, interconnect bandwidth, or combination thereof," according to the filing.

UBS Securities said that Russia and other US arms-embargo countries are part of the D5 group.

Nvidia's fiscal first-quarter results will include a charge of up to $5.5 billion related to inventory of H20 products, purchase commitments and related reserves, the company said. Nvidia's shares were falling 6.5% in Wednesday trading. The company's first quarter ends on April 27, and results for that period are due out May 28.

An inventory write-down of $5.5 billion likely indicates more than $12 billion in lost revenue with gross margins approaching 60%, Morgan Stanley said.

"We still expected H20 to be restricted by export controls, but stopped shipments 'effective immediately' is a bit more disruptive, and the large inventory write-down is a cautionary signal," the brokerage said.

The magnitude of the write-down suggests that management was more optimistic on future H20 revenue than what Morgan Stanley had perceived, according to the report. The bank cut Nvidia's data center revenue estimates by 8% to 9% for the next two quarters, but left its outlook unchanged beyond October.

"This is not a ban, it's a licensing requirement, but again the inventory write-down suggests that the company is not optimistic about being granted licenses," the note added.

After announcing sweeping new tariffs on US imports earlier this month, President Donald Trump declared a 90-day pause on certain tariffs for non-retaliating countries. China responded with its own retaliatory duties, and Washington and Beijing have since raised tariffs on each other's goods multiple times.

Separately, Advanced Micro Devices' said in a Wednesday filing that the new licensing requirement applies to its MI308 products, and may result in charges of up to $800 million.

Advanced Micro Devices' shares were down 6.6% intraday.

data.title: Dynavax, Deep Track Capital Step Up Fight Ahead of June 11 Annual Meeting
data.description:
Dynavax Technologies has again rejected efforts by activist investor Deep Track Capital to add several new directors to its board, keeping the two sides on a path to settle their fight at the biopharmaceutical company's upcoming annual shareholder's meeting in June.

Deep Track, which said it owns a 14.3% stake in Dynavax, said the company's board "has refused to compromise and seems set on not having a representative of one of its largest shareholders as a director."

Deep Track has been trying to influence operations at Dynavax since at least October 2022, demanding that the company suspend its acquisition strategy and double the size of its stock buyback program to $400 million, and has bolstered those demands by accumulating more Dynavax shares.

Deep Track, which has been seeking to name as many as four new directors to the nine-member board, made a compromise offer late last month that would have given it two board seats to replace two sitting directors, but that proposal was rejected, Deep Track said Wednesday.

Dynavax has issued preliminary documents scheduling its annual shareholders meeting for June 11 and reiterating its support for its four-person nominee slate. Shareholders of record on April 14 are eligible to participate, it said.

Dynavax did not immediately respond to a request from MT Newswires on Wednesday seeking comment.

data.title: General Dynamics says G800 earns FAA, EASA certifications
data.description:
General Dynamics said on Wednesday its Gulfstream G800 jet has earned a certification from the Federal Aviation Administration and the European Union Aviation Safety Agency.

The company had said in January it expected FAA certification for the jet in the first half of 2025.
